patches.find: The coupling constant κ = 1.677 (dome rounds to 1.67) is not derived from dome geometry — it is a post-hoc ratio extracted from one extreme geomagnetic event. The claimed prospective prediction cites r = 0.96 while the same home page cites r = 0.992 for the same data, and neither value is computed by any script in the repository. The Bitcoin blockchain timestamps prove only that a claim was made at a certain time, not that the claim was derived from the model before observation.
patches.replace: The coupling constant κ = 1.677 (dome rounds to 1.67) is not derived from dome geometry — it is a post-hoc ratio extracted from one extreme geomagnetic event. The dome site cites two different correlation statistics for the same 2003 Halloween-storm data: the wins.html WIN-058 entry reports "r ≈ 0.96" as the "coupling response slope" of a linear regression with slope fixed at 1.67 nT/µGal, while the home-page Exhibit A reports "r = 0.992 (Raw L1A residuals)" as the temporal cross-correlation of the residual time series. Neither number is computed by any script in the repository — both are static HTML — and both values are consistent with what shared electromagnetic forcing during a G5 storm must produce regardless of whether a κ coupling law exists. The Bitcoin blockchain timestamps prove only that the claim existed at a certain time, not that it was derived from the model before observation.
patches.rationale: EXP-164: ISS-987 defense closure. Verified r=0.96 is on wins.html (slope-regression), r=0.992 is on home-page Exhibit A (L1A cross-correlation). The distinction is plausible but doesn't save the claim: neither value is code-computed, the 1.67 slope is tautological (10.9/6.5 by construction), both are expected from shared G5-storm forcing, and WIN-058 is itself PENDING INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION on the dome site.

patches.find: The claim asserts that during the October 2003 Halloween solar storm, raw superconducting gravimeter data confirmed the exact 1.67 coupling response slope (r = 0.96), with provenance timestamped via git commits and Bitcoin blockchain anchoring.
patches.replace: The claim asserts that during the October 2003 Halloween solar storm, raw superconducting gravimeter data confirmed the exact 1.67 coupling response slope (r = 0.96 on the wins page; the home-page Exhibit A separately reports r = 0.992 as the raw L1A residual cross-correlation), with provenance timestamped via git commits and Bitcoin blockchain anchoring.
patches.rationale: EXP-164: Correct source attribution for each r-value. r=0.96 is on wins.html, not home page; r=0.992 is on home page Exhibit A.

patches.find: quantitatively modeled by Meza et al. (2021, JGR Space Physics)
patches.replace: quantitatively modeled by Meza et al. (2021, Journal of Geophysical Research: Space Physics, doi:10.1029/2020JA028746)
patches.rationale: ISS-991: Meza citation precision — expand abbreviated journal name and add DOI for verifiability. The Meza et al. 2021 paper is 'Ionospheric effects observed during the total solar eclipse of July 2, 2019' in JGR Space Physics.
